story:
Troy slid into his seat in the second row as the tardy bell faded, his chair tipping, then thumping onto all four legs. Mrs. B raised one eyebrow and began roll call. When the room tittered behind him, she paused. “Troy.” “Here.” “We know you’re here. What we don’t know is why everyone behind you is laughing.” He shrugged. She strolled down the aisle, feinted a glance out the window, and pointed. “What is that?” Half the class turned, including Troy. She bit back a smile: on the back of Troy’s head was a paper plate mask—grass-taped hair, one green crayon eye, one eye punched out with a black curl poking through, a purple button nose, a blue-outlined slit for a mouth with pointed sticks glued like tusks. “Troy Zimmer, take that mask off and give it to me.” He slipped the elastic over his head and handed it over. “Can I have it back? It’s my art project in the afterschool program.” Snickers: “The afterschool program from the summer, maybe.”

“It rests in my desk drawer until the end of day,” Mrs. B said. “Everyone, clear your desks. Blank paper, pencil, dictionary. For attendance today, we are defining decorum.” Groans drifted, a muttered “Way to go, Troy.” Names in the corner, definition copied, one sentence using the word. Pages flipped, pencils scratched, one thudded to the floor. Troy leaned too far to fish his pencil, rolling it finger to finger, considered rolling it under his chair until a look from Mrs. B changed his mind. He found the entry quickly, sat straight, feet tapping, shoulders swaying, carefully centered in his seat, and wrote: “Decorum is weird.”

Mrs. B glanced up to find some children quiet, some slouching, a few still writing—Troy leaning back, balancing his pencil on his lip. “Pass your papers to the right.” He shuffled the stack with flair, passed it along. “Here you go, Angeline.” “Please call me Angie.” “OK, Angel.” She groaned, nearly smiling. “You really should learn how to behave.” Before he could answer, Mrs. B called, “Open your math books to page five. We’re reviewing third grade this week; fourth-grade starts next week.” She caught Troy’s eye; he settled. 

Near the end of the class, Mrs. B said, “Let’s play a game. Count off to five—George, one; Angie, two—” Troy practically leaped when he yelled, “Four!”

The lunch bell rang. “Put your books away and line up.” “Alphabetically?” Angie asked. “Yes, Troy last,” George added. “Alphabetically, according to height,” Troy sang. Mrs. B shook her head. “Let’s march. Backs straight, shoulders back, arms at your sides, mouths shut, eyes open.” The line wasn’t perfect, but it moved.

At 3:16 the dismissal bell sent backpacks flying. Troy lingered at the desk. “Can I have my mask back?” Mrs. B held it, not releasing. “You’re very kinetic, Troy. We need to help you not distract others. How can we do that?” He met her eyes. “I do my best, ma’am, but it’s like I have a bag of balls inside and they’re always bouncing.” She softened. “You’re not a bad kid. I see you trying. You do like making the other kids laugh, though?” He grinned. “I do. It helps whatever it is inside.” Then, worried: “You aren’t going to make me take medication, are you? They tried that at my last school. I was like the walking dead. I wasn’t me.”

“I’m not trying to get you on medication,” she said. “There’s a new martial arts class in the afterschool program.” She handed him a flyer and the mask. He perched the mask on his head. “What’s martial arts?” “A system to focus your energy—jumping, kicking, later maybe throwing people, but we start with moves and focus.” He scanned the paper. “You’re the teacher?!” “Yes. I think you’ll do well.” “How do you know?” “I was a kid always in motion too. I’m still in motion. Martial arts helps aim that energy.” “Kinetic means motion, doesn’t it?” “Yes. Will I see you Thursday? I can call your mom.” “She won’t mind. I just want to keep art too.” “That shouldn’t be a problem.” He waved the paper, ran out, then skidded into a fast walk. “See you tomorrow.”
